A. Perception

1. Understanding Images
• Grayscale images: single channel of intensity values from 0 to 255 in a 2D matrix.
• Color images: multiple channels (RGB) with intensity values from 0 to 255, represented by three 2D matrices.
• Image characteristics:
	+ Resolution: number of pixels.
	+ Bit Depth: number of bits per pixel.
	+ Noise: unwanted artifacts or variations.

Mathematical operations on matrices are used for image understanding, including edge detection and texture analysis. This is where Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) come into play.

2. CNNs
• Neural networks with convolutional layers instead of fully connected layers.
• Kernels sweep over input matrix to produce feature maps.
• Output calculated as weighted sum of kernel entries and input entries, adding multiple channels if present.
• Convolutional layers learn image features from simple shapes to complex objects.

A. Perception
1. Understanding Images
In computer vision, an image can be either grayscale or color, depending on whether it contains information
about light intensity alone or the combination of different colors. The images are composed of pixels, where
each pixel has an intensity value. These images are stored as matrices, where:
● A grayscale image consists of a single channel of intensity values ranging from black to white. This
means it is represented as a 2D matrix with pixel values from 0(black) to 255(white).
● A color image has multiple channels, each representing a different color channel such as red, green,
and blue (RGB). They are represented by three 2D matrices, one for each color channel. Their
intensity values also vary from 0 to 255.
Some image characteristics are:
● Resolution: The resolution of an image refers to the number of pixels it contains. A higher
resolution image will contain more pixels and thus more detail.
● Bit Depth: Bit depth refers to the number of bits used to represent each pixel in an image. A higher
bit depth allows for more color and brightness variations in an image (since the number of possible
intensity values that can be represented increase).
● Noise: Noise refers to any unwanted artifacts or variations in an image. It can be caused by factors
such as sensor sensitivity, lighting conditions, or image compression.
For computers, understanding images requires mathematical operations on these matrices. These
mathematical operations help the computer in detecting edges, textures, corners etc. This is where
Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) come into play.
2. CNNs
As seen below, CNNs are neural networks where we have convolutional layers instead of fully connected
layers. Convolutional layers consist of “kernels,” which are matrices. The output of each layer is another
matrix known as feature maps, whose entries are calculated as the sum of input entries weighted by kernel
entries. The kernel sweeps over the entire input matrix (the image is represented as a matrix). In the case
of multiple channels, the weighted sums of each channel with its kernel are added to give output. The
convolutional layers learn features of images from simple things such as edges or some shapes to more
complicated things like facial features or complete objects.

3. Object Detection
Object detection involves identifying and localizing objects in an image. For localization, bounding boxes
are drawn around the identified object. Object detection algorithms employ a combination of techniques
to accomplish their tasks effectively. They utilize Region Proposal Networks (RPNs) to generate potential
object proposals. These proposals are then analyzed by feature extraction networks, which extract relevant
features to accurately classify and localize objects within the image or video.
Some popular object detection models include:
● Faster R-CNN (Region-based Convolutional Neural Network) : Uses a Region Proposal Network
(RPN) to efficiently detect objects with high accuracy.
● YOLO (You Only Look Once) : A fast and real-time object detection model that processes an image
in a single pass.
4. Object Segmentation
Object Segmentation or image segmentation refers to categorizing each pixel value of an image to a
particular class. This results in a pixel-wise mask for the objects. Types include:
● Semantic Segmentation: Classifies pixels into categories (e.g., road, car, pedestrian).
● Instance Segmentation: Differentiates between multiple instances of the same object (e.g., two
different cars).

5. Object Tracking
Object tracking is the process of locating and following an object's movement in a video or a sequence of
frames. Object tracking aims to identify an object of interest in the first frame and track it as it moves in
subsequent frames. The object tracking process is terminated when the object of interest is no longer
visible or the tracking algorithm fails to track the object accurately.
It is crucial for autonomous driving to track objects that need to be avoided (like other cars, pedestrians, or
any other obstacles) or followed(lane boundaries need to be tracked to ensure the vehicle stays in the lane)
.
Some common problems in object racking include:

● Occlusion: It is known as occlusion when the object is hidden partially or entirely due to other
objects. Solutions include using multiple detectors to detect different parts or a Kalman filter to
predict the expected position of the object based on its motion in the previous frame.
● Motion blur: Sometimes, objects can appear blurred in video frames due to fast motion. To handle
this Kalman filter can be used to track using the expected position until a sharp object is found
again.
6. LIDAR (Light Detection and Ranging) sensor
Cameras provide 2D images of the environment, but autonomous cars need 3D information like the
distance of objects from the vehicle or the velocity of other objects to make decisions, such as when to
turn or stop. LIDAR sensor is used for this purpose:
● Emits laser pulses and measures the reflected time to compute distance (like sonar, except it uses
light pulses instead of sound waves, which are significantly slower).
● Generates a 3D point cloud representing the surroundings.
● It helps in depth and velocity estimation, which is critical for navigation.
7. Stereo Camera

A stereo camera uses two or more image sensors to simulate human binocular vision, allowing it to perceive depth through Stereo disparity. This is the difference in image location of an object seen by left and right eyes due to horizontal separation. Stereo disparity can be found using two 2D images from different positions, with sufficient details and texture for correlation-based depth image creation. Suitable for large field of view applications and outdoor usage.
level_1.original_content:
IIT Roorkee Motorsports Autonomous –
Recruitment 2026
A stereo camera is a type of camera with two or more image sensors. This allows the
camera to simulate human binocular vision and therefore gives it the ability to perceive
depth.
The human binocular vision perceives depth by using Stereo disparity which refers to
the difference in image location of an object seen by the left and right eyes, resulting
from the eyes’ horizontal separation.
The brain uses this binocular disparity to extract depth information from the two-
dimensional retinal images which are known as stereopsis.
Stereo Disparity in a camera can be found by using two 2D images taken from different
positions and the correlation between the images can be used to create a depth image.
However, to find correlations the two images need to have sufficient details and texture
or non-uniformity.
Thus, the Stereo vision is suitable for applications with a large field of view and for
outdoor usage.

1. ORB (Oriented FAST and Rotated BRIEF)
ORB is a fast, efficient algorithm designed to detect "keypoints" (interesting features) in
an image and describe them so they can be matched across different frames. It was
created as a computationally efficient, open-source alternative to SIFT and SURF.
ORB is actually a fusion of two other algorithms, modified to handle rotation:
1. Feature Detector: Oriented FAST (oFAST)
2. Feature Descriptor: Rotated BRIEF (rBRIEF)

B. SLAM
1. Introduction to SLAM
https://www.mathworks.com/discovery/slam.html
https://www.mdpi.com/2072-4292/15/4/1156 (Intensive one)
Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) is a groundbreaking technology that allows autonomous
systems, such as robots or vehicles, to navigate and understand unfamiliar environments. It achieves this
by simultaneously creating a map of the surroundings and determining the system's position within that
map. This dual challenge was once considered a "chicken-or-egg" problem in robotics, as mapping requires
knowing the location, and localization requires a map. SLAM elegantly solves this interdependency.
In essence, SLAM enables machines to explore unknown spaces much like humans do when navigating
new places by identifying landmarks and using them as reference points.
For example:
● A robotic vacuum cleaner uses SLAM to map your house while cleaning, ensuring it doesn’t miss
spots or repeatedly clean the same area.
● A self-driving car uses SLAM to navigate roads safely by continuously updating its map of the
surroundings.

2. Core Components of SLAM:
I. Range-Bearing Measurement & Prediction: uses cameras, image sensors, LiDAR, sonar, and other devices for spatial property detection.
II. Data Processing and Extraction: processes data from accelerometers, positional observance, and other sources to calculate position, acceleration, velocity, and yaw.
III. Data Association and Mapping: relates new observed frames with preceding ones based on estimates to determine vehicle position; accommodates gating regions for simplified calculations.
IV. Loop Closure: recognizes previously visited areas to correct localization or mapping errors.

2. Core Components of SLAM
SLAM systems are built on two essential pillars:
I. Range-Bearing Measurement & Prediction
Every SLAM system relies on a sensing mechanism that enables a robot or vehicle to perceive and measure
its surroundings. This is achieved through various technologies, including cameras, image sensors, LiDAR
(Light Detection and Ranging), and sonar. Essentially, any device capable of detecting spatial properties
such as location, distance, and velocity can serve as a range measurement tool within a SLAM framework.
II. Data Processing and Extraction
Once environmental measurements are collected, the SLAM system employs computational methods to
process and interpret this data. The type of data ranges from the accelerometer readings from the car’s
body to the positional observance of objects surrounding the vehicle. The primary objective of this data
extraction process is to obtain the necessary data to help aid the calculations of position, acceleration,
velocity and yaw.
A well-integrated SLAM system continuously synchronizes its sensing hardware, data processing
algorithms, and the movement of the robot or vehicle itself. This interplay ensures that the system can
accurately explore and navigate its surroundings. While the specific components and technologies used
may vary depending on the application, their seamless coordination is essential for the system’s
effectiveness.
III. Data Association and Mapping
Reference: https://web.mit.edu/2.166/www/handouts/2166_data_association_jose_neira.pdf
Data Association is one of the key features governing a SLAM algorithm. To accurately map and recognise
its own position within the virtual world, the algorithm must relate each new observed frame with the
preceding one based on estimates to determine where the vehicle should be after the duration of time
between the observed frames. Many such algorithms even accommodate the use of gating regions, in an
attempt to simplify the association calculations.
IV. Loop Closure
Example- https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lThcTKYE7Es&t=147s
Loop closure is a critical feature of SLAM that ensures accuracy over time. It involves recognizing
previously visited areas and correcting any drift in localization or mapping errors.
For example: Imagine a robot exploring a building. When it revisits a hallway it mapped earlier, loop closure
ensures that any discrepancies between its current position and the earlier map are corrected.

3. SLAM Techniques for Motorsports based Navigation
I. LIDAR-Only SLAM
LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging) is a popular choice for SLAM in motorsports due to its ability to
provide precise 3D point cloud data. This data enables the detection and localization of objects like traffic
cones, which are essential for defining the racetrack.
Steps in LiDAR SLAM for Cone Detection
● Preprocessing Point Cloud Data:
■ Field of View Trimming: Limits the sensor's range to focus only on relevant areas.
■ Ground Plane Segmentation: Separates the ground from objects like cones to simplify
detection.
● Clustering Potential Obstacles:
■ Groups point in the cloud that likely belong to the same object (e.g., a cone).
● Key Techniques:
■ Cylindrical Volume Reconstruction: Ensures all points within a cylindrical region around
each cluster are included.
■ Filtering Based on Expected Point Counts: Verifies if the number of points in a cluster
matches the expected size of a cone.
● Scan Matching Algorithms:
■ These algorithms align consecutive LiDAR scans to maintain accurate localization:
■ - Iterative Closest Point (ICP): Matches corresponding points between scans to minimize
positional errors.
Videos - https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4uWSo8v3iQA
■ - Normal Distribution Transform (NDT): Models point clouds as Gaussian distributions,
making them robust against noise.
Blog - https://medium.com/towards-data-science/types-of-transformations-for-better-normal-
distribution-61c22668d3b9
II. General Architecture of LIDAR + Wheel Odometry
For motorsports navigation, combining LiDAR with wheel odometry enhances accuracy. Here's how it
works:
● Wheel odometry provides initial estimates of the vehicle's movement based on wheel rotations.
● LiDAR refines these estimates by comparing real-world measurements with the map being built.

IV. EKF-SLAM
EKF-SLAM estimates both the robot's position and the locations of landmarks (e.g., cones) in the
environment. It alternates between:
● Prediction: Uses the robot's motion model to predict its next position, increasing uncertainty due
to process noise.
● Correction: Updates the prediction using observed landmarks, reducing uncertainty by comparing
predicted and actual sensor measurements.
For example, a robot navigating a cone-marked track predicts its movement using odometry and refines its
position when it observes a cone landmark.
Variations of EKF-SLAM for Non-Linear Trends : First Estimates Jacobian EKF (FEJ-EKF), Iterated EKF
(IEKF), Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF), Submap-Based EKF-SLAM, Dynamic SLAM Variants.
Other such variations include a graph based SLAM or a Particle Filter Based SLAM involving algorithms like
Monte Carlo Simulations or a field based recursion.
You can refer to this article for more concreteness in EKF-SLAM -
https://jihongju.github.io/2018/10/05/slam-05-ekf-slam/
Do check out this video to understand how randomness exists via a Monte Carlo Simulation to build
intuition:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7ESK5SaP-bc
And also explore this approach if interested in diving deeper into how SLAM utilises it:
https://robots.stanford.edu/papers/Thrun03g.pdf
4. Deep Learning in SLAM
Deep Learning (DL) has emerged as a transformative tool in Simultaneous Localization and Mapping
(SLAM), offering data-driven solutions to challenges that traditional geometry-based approaches struggle
with. By leveraging neural networks, SLAM systems can achieve greater robustness, adaptability, and
efficiency, especially in dynamic or complex environments. Below, we scratch the surface of this exciting
intersection by exploring key architectures, challenges, and their potential fixes.
Reinforcement learning can also see scope among the many existing algorithms for high speed localisation

C. Path Planning
1. What is Path Planning?
Path planning determines a safe, efficient, and feasible trajectory from starting position to desired destination.

2. Importance of Path Planning in Autonomous Vehicles
Effective path planning impacts safety, efficiency, and performance. A well-planned path ensures:
• Safety: Avoids collisions with obstacles.
• Efficiency: Finds optimal route based on criteria like travel time, energy consumption, or passenger comfort.
• Adaptability: Responds to real-time changes.

Path planning is categorized into two types:
• Global planning: High-level route based on surroundings and track, assuming a known, static environment.
• Local planning: Real-time navigation and obstacle avoidance on the track, reacting to environmental changes.

3. Overview of Path Planning Algorithms

C. Path Planning
1. What is Path Planning?
Path planning is a fundamental component of autonomous vehicle systems that determines a safe,
efficient, and feasible trajectory for a vehicle from its starting position to a desired destination. The primary
goal is to navigate through a dynamic environment while avoiding obstacles, adhering to traffic rules, and
optimizing factors such as time, energy, and comfort. It is the process of determining the optimal route
from a starting point to a destination while avoiding obstacles and adhering to rules and regulations.
2. Importance of Path Planning in Autonomous Vehicles
For autonomous vehicles, effective path planning is crucial because it directly impacts safety, efficiency,
and overall performance. A well-planned path ensures:
● Safety: Avoids collisions with static and dynamic obstacles.
● Efficiency: Finds the shortest or most optimal route based on criteria like travel time, energy
consumption, or passenger comfort.
● Adaptability: Responds to real-time changes in traffic, road conditions, or unpredictable obstacles.
Path planning can be broadly categorized into two types based on the level of planning and how dynamic
the environment is:
● Global planning: A high-level route between the source to destination based on the surroundings
and track of the autonomous vehicle. It assumes a known, static environment and does not
account for real-time changes such as moving obstacles or traffic conditions.
● Local planning: Handling real-time navigation and obstacle avoidance on the track. Unlike global
planning, as the name suggests, it reacts to changes in the environment as the vehicle moves.

I. Artificial Potential Field (APF)
The Artificial Potential Field (APF) method is a local path planning technique widely
used in autonomous vehicles and robotics for real-time obstacle avoidance. It models
the environment using a mathematical field where the goal exerts an attractive force,
and obstacles exert repulsive forces. The vehicle navigates by following the resultant
force, ensuring a smooth and safe trajectory.
Reference: Predictive Artificial Potential Field algorithm - energy-efficient local path
planning algorithm
II. Rapidly-Exploring Random Tree

paths to find the optimal path). It allows efficient exploration of
high-dimensional spaces by incrementally building a tree
structure.
It consists of three steps, viz. exploration, obstacle avoidance
and path formation:
Reference: RRT* Algorithm Explained
III. A* and D* Algorithms
Both A* and D* are heuristic-based path planning algorithms widely used in robotics, AI, and autonomous
vehicles. A* is used for static environments, while D* is an extension designed for dynamic, changing
environments.
A* Algorithm
● A* is a heuristic-based graph-based search algorithm that finds the shortest path from a start node
to a goal node. It combines concepts from Dijkstra’s Algorithm (which finds the shortest path but
explores too many nodes) and Greedy Best-First Search (which is fast but may not always find the
best path)
D* (Dynamic A*) Algorithm
● D* is an extension of A* designed for dynamic environments where obstacles may change after
path computation. It is widely used in robotics and autonomous vehicles to replan paths when new
obstacles appear.

Delaunay triangulation and Voronoi diagrams are geometric
structures used in computational geometry and robotics. They help
in path planning, obstacle avoidance, and spatial analysis in
autonomous vehicles in simple track situations like those in FS AI.
Voronoi Diagram
A Voronoi diagram partitions a plane into regions based on proximity
to a given set of points. Each region contains all points that are
closer to one specific point than to any other.
Delaunay Triangulation
Delaunay triangulation is a way of connecting a set of points with
non-overlapping triangles such that the circumcircle of each triangle
contains no other points. It is essentially equivalent to KNN with k=1
(ML algorithm).

RNNs and LSTMs are some architectures, alongside RL algorithms that can be used for various parts of
path planning. RL is increasingly used to replace the "Local Planner" or "Controller"—the component that
decides how to steer right now to stay on the optimal path calculated by the global planner.

D. Control
1. Introduction
“How do we make the car follow the planned path accurately?”
Self-driving automobiles, sometimes referred to as autonomous vehicles, are a cutting-edge
technological advancement that could revolutionize our daily lives and modes of transportation. These
cars mimic a human driver’s decision-making process by utilizing a combination of sensors, software,
and control systems to make decisions in real time. They are designed to function alone and navigate.
Control theory is the study of how to apply force to an object to control its movement. For self-driving
cars, we can apply forces to the vehicle via three "control inputs": steering, throttle, and brake.
The algorithms that determine how to use these control inputs are called "controllers". There are many,
many types of controllers, although in the vehicle domain only a few are common and the rest are more
specialized. See references for more control algorithms.
● The control system receives a desired value, such as the target speed or steering angle, and also
measures the vehicle’s actual speed or steering using sensors.
● It compares the desired value with the actual value to calculate the error between them.
● A controller processes this error and decides how much steering, throttle, or braking action is
required.
● These control commands are sent to the vehicle, and the car moves according to physical effects
like tire grip and load transfer.
● Sensors measure the new vehicle state and send feedback to the controller, and this process
repeats continuously to keep the vehicle stable and accurate.

PID Controller:
• Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controller with K_p, K_i, and K_d constants.
• Feedback control method to reach and maintain desired values (speed or steering angle).
• Calculates error by comparing desired value with actual value measured by sensors.

• I part: removes steady-state error by accumulating error over time.
• D part: predicts future errors by observing error change, reducing overshoot and oscillations.
• Combined P, I, and D actions generate control output for steering, throttle, or braking.

● PID is one of the most commonly used and robust controllers out there. It stands for Proportional-
Integral-Derivative Controller. We have 3 constants here : namely K_p, K_i, K_d, each controlling the
proportional, integral, and derivative actions respectively.
● PID control is a feedback control method used to make a system reach and maintain a desired
value, such as speed or steering angle.
● The controller first calculates the error by comparing the desired value with the actual value
measured by sensors.
● The Proportional (P) part reacts to the current error and applies a correction that is directly
proportional to how big the error is.
● The Integral (I) part looks at the accumulated error over time and helps remove any steady-state
error that remains after proportional control.
● The Derivative (D) part predicts future errors by observing how fast the error is changing and
helps reduce overshoot and oscillations.
● The combined P, I, and D actions generate a control output that adjusts steering, throttle, or
braking.
● The controller continuously repeats this process using feedback, so the system remains stable
and accurate.

●The steering effort required by the driver should not be too much to make them uncomfortable.
●The steering system needs to be such that the car can maneuver all turns on the track.
II. Types of Steering Systems
Parallel - Geometry -
The simplest steering system that can be designed is Parallel steering, which suggests that both the front
wheels will turn at an identical angle.
Ackermann - Geometry -
The typical steering system in a road car is the Ackermann steering system. It is governed by the
Ackermann principle, which prevents the tyres from slipping sideways while turning. To turn about an
instantaneous centre, the inner wheel angle is greater than the outer wheel angle to turn about an
instantaneous centre.
Anti-Ackerman Geometry -
Anti Ackermann geometry is the reverse of Ackermann geometry. The outer wheel angle is greater than
the inner wheel angle. This type of geometry is preferred by most racing cars.

4. Braking
Braking is the process of slowing down or stopping a vehicle. A hydraulic braking system (like the one used
in most cars with disc brakes) works as follows:
● Pressing the Brake Pedal
■ When you press the brake pedal, you apply a force.
■ This force is amplified by the pedal ratio (P) to make braking easier.
● Hydraulic System Activation
■ The force is transmitted to the master cylinder, which contains brake fluid.
■ This fluid gets compressed and travels through hydraulic lines to the brake calipers.
● Brake Caliper and Disc Interaction
■ The brake calipers have pistons that push brake pads against the rotating brake disc
(rotor).
■ This creates friction, which slows down the wheel.
● Stopping the Vehicle
■ The friction between the brake pads and disc produces braking torque (opposite to wheel
rotation).
■ This torque helps bring the vehicle to a stop.
● Releasing the Brakes
■ When you release the brake pedal, the pressure in the hydraulic system decreases.
■ The pistons move back, and the brake pads stop pressing the disc, allowing the wheels to
rotate freely.

2. Powertrain Configurations
Electric vehicles (EVs) use different motor configurations to power their wheels. Here's a simplified
explanation of three common setups:
I. Single Motor Configuration
Only one motor powers either the front or rear axle (axle: shaft connecting to both left and right wheels);
and uses a differential to distribute power and torque between the left and right wheel. This setup is simple
and cost-effective, ideal for everyday driving. However, it offers less control over traction and cornering.
● Simpler control algorithms focus on acceleration and braking.
● Limited capability for advanced traction control.
Differential: A differential allows wheels on the same axle to rotate at different speeds, improving cornering
by compensating for the varying distances each wheel travels during turns. Nowadays, there are
differentials that can be electronically and algorithmically controlled for torque/power distribution.

I. Motor Control Unit
● Throttle input in vehicles translates to motor commands by interpreting the driver's acceleration
demand. Sensors capture the throttle position, and the Electronic Control Unit (ECU) processes the
signal. It varies the current/voltage/power output to change motor’s speed and torque based on
throttle input.
● Further, the Motor Control Unit (MCU) transforms the battery’s direct current into alternating
current using a Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) by applying pulse width modulated (PWM) waveforms
to the gates of VSI switches/MOSFETs. PWM pulses are generated according to the position
sensors (Hall/Encoder/Resolver) feedback and the ON Time/Duty Cycle of these pulses varies
according to the required Speed/Torque command. To achieve efficient closed loop control of
Motor drive, Vector Control methods/Field Oriented Control (FOC) are adopted.

G. Robot Operating System (ROS)
1. ROS and its use in Autonomous Vehicles:
ROS is a flexible framework that helps developers build robot applications. It is not actually an operating
system, rather a collection of tools, libraries, and conventions that simplify the complex task of creating
robust robot behaviors.
Autonomous vehicles need to handle multiple tasks simultaneously - they need to understand their
environment through sensors, plan their path, control their movement, and make split-second decisions.
ROS provides the infrastructure to manage all these tasks efficiently. For example, one component might
process camera data to detect obstacles, while another plans the safest route, and yet another controls
the steering and acceleration.
ROS2 is particularly well-suited for autonomous vehicles because it can handle the demanding
requirements of real-time processing and safety-critical operations. ROS2 has a highly sophisticated
modular software architecture where different functionalities (e.g., sensor fusion, decision-making, and
motion control) interact smoothly

Now, let's look at the core components of ROS2:
ROS2 follows a distributed computing model, where different components run as independent nodes.
These nodes communicate through:
● Topics → For continuous data streaming (e.g., LiDAR data).
● Services → For request-response operations.
● Actions → For long-running tasks like motion planning.
A term you will read a lot is the Publisher-Subscriber Mode
Message passing in ROS happens with the Publisher Subscriber Interface provided by ROS library
functions.
A ROS Node can be a Publisher or a Subscriber. A Publisher is the one that puts the messages of some
standard Message Type to a particular Topic. The Subscriber on the other hand subscribes to the Topic so
that it receives the messages whenever any message is published to the Topic.

A. Perception
1. Understanding Images
In computer vision, an image can be either grayscale or color, depending on whether it contains information
about light intensity alone or the combination of different colors. The images are composed of pixels, where
each pixel has an intensity value. These images are stored as matrices, where:
● A grayscale image consists of a single channel of intensity values ranging from black to white. This
means it is represented as a 2D matrix with pixel values from 0(black) to 255(white).
● A color image has multiple channels, each representing a different color channel such as red, green,
and blue (RGB). They are represented by three 2D matrices, one for each color channel. Their
intensity values also vary from 0 to 255.
Some image characteristics are:
● Resolution: The resolution of an image refers to the number of pixels it contains. A higher
resolution image will contain more pixels and thus more detail.
● Bit Depth: Bit depth refers to the number of bits used to represent each pixel in an image. A higher
bit depth allows for more color and brightness variations in an image (since the number of possible
intensity values that can be represented increase).
● Noise: Noise refers to any unwanted artifacts or variations in an image. It can be caused by factors
such as sensor sensitivity, lighting conditions, or image compression.
For computers, understanding images requires mathematical operations on these matrices. These
mathematical operations help the computer in detecting edges, textures, corners etc. This is where
Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) come into play.
2. CNNs
As seen below, CNNs are neural networks where we have convolutional layers instead of fully connected
layers. Convolutional layers consist of “kernels,” which are matrices. The output of each layer is another
matrix known as feature maps, whose entries are calculated as the sum of input entries weighted by kernel
entries. The kernel sweeps over the entire input matrix (the image is represented as a matrix). In the case
of multiple channels, the weighted sums of each channel with its kernel are added to give output. The
convolutional layers learn features of images from simple things such as edges or some shapes to more
complicated things like facial features or complete objects.

IIT Roorkee Motorsports Autonomous –
Recruitment 2026
CNNs also consist of pooling layers and fully connected layers. Pooling layers are used to reduce the
dimensionality of feature maps. Fully connected layers are generally added at the network's end to predict
class probabilities in object identification tasks.
Most vision architectures use convolutions as a fundamental building block.

IIT Roorkee Motorsports Autonomous –
Recruitment 2026
3. Object Detection
Object detection involves identifying and localizing objects in an image. For localization, bounding boxes
are drawn around the identified object. Object detection algorithms employ a combination of techniques
to accomplish their tasks effectively. They utilize Region Proposal Networks (RPNs) to generate potential
object proposals. These proposals are then analyzed by feature extraction networks, which extract relevant
features to accurately classify and localize objects within the image or video.
Some popular object detection models include:
● Faster R-CNN (Region-based Convolutional Neural Network) : Uses a Region Proposal Network
(RPN) to efficiently detect objects with high accuracy.
● YOLO (You Only Look Once) : A fast and real-time object detection model that processes an image
in a single pass.
4. Object Segmentation
Object Segmentation or image segmentation refers to categorizing each pixel value of an image to a
particular class. This results in a pixel-wise mask for the objects. Types include:
● Semantic Segmentation: Classifies pixels into categories (e.g., road, car, pedestrian).
● Instance Segmentation: Differentiates between multiple instances of the same object (e.g., two
different cars).

IIT Roorkee Motorsports Autonomous –
Recruitment 2026
5. Object Tracking
Object tracking is the process of locating and following an object's movement in a video or a sequence of
frames. Object tracking aims to identify an object of interest in the first frame and track it as it moves in
subsequent frames. The object tracking process is terminated when the object of interest is no longer
visible or the tracking algorithm fails to track the object accurately.
It is crucial for autonomous driving to track objects that need to be avoided (like other cars, pedestrians, or
any other obstacles) or followed(lane boundaries need to be tracked to ensure the vehicle stays in the lane)
.
Some common problems in object racking include:

IIT Roorkee Motorsports Autonomous –
Recruitment 2026
● Occlusion: It is known as occlusion when the object is hidden partially or entirely due to other
objects. Solutions include using multiple detectors to detect different parts or a Kalman filter to
predict the expected position of the object based on its motion in the previous frame.
● Motion blur: Sometimes, objects can appear blurred in video frames due to fast motion. To handle
this Kalman filter can be used to track using the expected position until a sharp object is found
again.
6. LIDAR (Light Detection and Ranging) sensor
Cameras provide 2D images of the environment, but autonomous cars need 3D information like the
distance of objects from the vehicle or the velocity of other objects to make decisions, such as when to
turn or stop. LIDAR sensor is used for this purpose:
● Emits laser pulses and measures the reflected time to compute distance (like sonar, except it uses
light pulses instead of sound waves, which are significantly slower).
● Generates a 3D point cloud representing the surroundings.
● It helps in depth and velocity estimation, which is critical for navigation.
7. Stereo Camera

IIT Roorkee Motorsports Autonomous –
Recruitment 2026
A stereo camera is a type of camera with two or more image sensors. This allows the
camera to simulate human binocular vision and therefore gives it the ability to perceive
depth.
The human binocular vision perceives depth by using Stereo disparity which refers to
the difference in image location of an object seen by the left and right eyes, resulting
from the eyes’ horizontal separation.
The brain uses this binocular disparity to extract depth information from the two-
dimensional retinal images which are known as stereopsis.
Stereo Disparity in a camera can be found by using two 2D images taken from different
positions and the correlation between the images can be used to create a depth image.
However, to find correlations the two images need to have sufficient details and texture
or non-uniformity.
Thus, the Stereo vision is suitable for applications with a large field of view and for
outdoor usage.

ORB is a fast, efficient algorithm designed to detect "keypoints" (interesting features) in
an image and describe them so they can be matched across different frames. It was
created as a computationally efficient, open-source alternative to SIFT and SURF.
ORB is actually a fusion of two other algorithms, modified to handle rotation:
1. Feature Detector: Oriented FAST (oFAST)
2. Feature Descriptor: Rotated BRIEF (rBRIEF)

B. SLAM
1. Introduction to SLAM
https://www.mathworks.com/discovery/slam.html
https://www.mdpi.com/2072-4292/15/4/1156 (Intensive one)
Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) is a groundbreaking technology that allows autonomous
systems, such as robots or vehicles, to navigate and understand unfamiliar environments. It achieves this
by simultaneously creating a map of the surroundings and determining the system's position within that
map. This dual challenge was once considered a "chicken-or-egg" problem in robotics, as mapping requires
knowing the location, and localization requires a map. SLAM elegantly solves this interdependency.
In essence, SLAM enables machines to explore unknown spaces much like humans do when navigating
new places by identifying landmarks and using them as reference points.
For example:
● A robotic vacuum cleaner uses SLAM to map your house while cleaning, ensuring it doesn’t miss
spots or repeatedly clean the same area.
● A self-driving car uses SLAM to navigate roads safely by continuously updating its map of the
surroundings.

IIT Roorkee Motorsports Autonomous –
Recruitment 2026
2. Core Components of SLAM
SLAM systems are built on two essential pillars:
I. Range-Bearing Measurement & Prediction
Every SLAM system relies on a sensing mechanism that enables a robot or vehicle to perceive and measure
its surroundings. This is achieved through various technologies, including cameras, image sensors, LiDAR
(Light Detection and Ranging), and sonar. Essentially, any device capable of detecting spatial properties
such as location, distance, and velocity can serve as a range measurement tool within a SLAM framework.
II. Data Processing and Extraction
Once environmental measurements are collected, the SLAM system employs computational methods to
process and interpret this data. The type of data ranges from the accelerometer readings from the car’s
body to the positional observance of objects surrounding the vehicle. The primary objective of this data
extraction process is to obtain the necessary data to help aid the calculations of position, acceleration,
velocity and yaw.
A well-integrated SLAM system continuously synchronizes its sensing hardware, data processing
algorithms, and the movement of the robot or vehicle itself. This interplay ensures that the system can
accurately explore and navigate its surroundings. While the specific components and technologies used
may vary depending on the application, their seamless coordination is essential for the system’s
effectiveness.
III. Data Association and Mapping
Reference: https://web.mit.edu/2.166/www/handouts/2166_data_association_jose_neira.pdf
Data Association is one of the key features governing a SLAM algorithm. To accurately map and recognise
its own position within the virtual world, the algorithm must relate each new observed frame with the
preceding one based on estimates to determine where the vehicle should be after the duration of time
between the observed frames. Many such algorithms even accommodate the use of gating regions, in an
attempt to simplify the association calculations.
IV. Loop Closure
Example- https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lThcTKYE7Es&t=147s
Loop closure is a critical feature of SLAM that ensures accuracy over time. It involves recognizing
previously visited areas and correcting any drift in localization or mapping errors.
For example: Imagine a robot exploring a building. When it revisits a hallway it mapped earlier, loop closure
ensures that any discrepancies between its current position and the earlier map are corrected.

S6TyGrmcA2X7kg&index=3
3. SLAM Techniques for Motorsports based Navigation
I. LIDAR-Only SLAM
LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging) is a popular choice for SLAM in motorsports due to its ability to
provide precise 3D point cloud data. This data enables the detection and localization of objects like traffic
cones, which are essential for defining the racetrack.
Steps in LiDAR SLAM for Cone Detection
● Preprocessing Point Cloud Data:
■ Field of View Trimming: Limits the sensor's range to focus only on relevant areas.
■ Ground Plane Segmentation: Separates the ground from objects like cones to simplify
detection.
● Clustering Potential Obstacles:
■ Groups point in the cloud that likely belong to the same object (e.g., a cone).
● Key Techniques:
■ Cylindrical Volume Reconstruction: Ensures all points within a cylindrical region around
each cluster are included.
■ Filtering Based on Expected Point Counts: Verifies if the number of points in a cluster
matches the expected size of a cone.
● Scan Matching Algorithms:
■ These algorithms align consecutive LiDAR scans to maintain accurate localization:
■ - Iterative Closest Point (ICP): Matches corresponding points between scans to minimize
positional errors.
Videos - https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4uWSo8v3iQA
■ - Normal Distribution Transform (NDT): Models point clouds as Gaussian distributions,
making them robust against noise.
Blog - https://medium.com/towards-data-science/types-of-transformations-for-better-normal-
distribution-61c22668d3b9
II. General Architecture of LIDAR + Wheel Odometry
For motorsports navigation, combining LiDAR with wheel odometry enhances accuracy. Here's how it
works:
● Wheel odometry provides initial estimates of the vehicle's movement based on wheel rotations.
● LiDAR refines these estimates by comparing real-world measurements with the map being built.

IIT Roorkee Motorsports Autonomous –
Recruitment 2026
This fusion ensures precise localization even at high speeds or during sharp turns.
reference - https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/stamp/stamp.jsp?tp=&arnumber=9636299)
III. VISUAL SLAM
Visual SLAM uses cameras instead of LiDAR to detect and map objects. It is often combined with LiDAR
for enhanced performance in motorsports.
Feature-Based Methods:
● Extracts distinct features (e.g., corners or edges) from images.
● Tracks these features across frames to estimate motion and reconstruct the environment.
● Example: ORB-SLAM, which uses ORB (Oriented FAST and Rotated BRIEF) features for real-time
mapping.(examples -
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=djZKOmNs7sA&list=PL1nA2S3vm3wBQFuLKzq1FdLLIjn4Na
GEf)
Direct Methods:
● Uses all pixel intensities instead of specific features.
● Estimates motion by comparing brightness values between consecutive frames.
● Works well in environments with minimal texture but is sensitive to lighting changes.
Advantages:
● Cost-effective compared to LiDAR.
● Provides rich visual information, such as cone color or texture.
Challenges:
● Less reliable in low-light or high-speed scenarios without additional processing.
● Computationally demanding when producing dense maps.

IIT Roorkee Motorsports Autonomous –
Recruitment 2026
IV. EKF-SLAM
EKF-SLAM estimates both the robot's position and the locations of landmarks (e.g., cones) in the
environment. It alternates between:
● Prediction: Uses the robot's motion model to predict its next position, increasing uncertainty due
to process noise.
● Correction: Updates the prediction using observed landmarks, reducing uncertainty by comparing
predicted and actual sensor measurements.
For example, a robot navigating a cone-marked track predicts its movement using odometry and refines its
position when it observes a cone landmark.
Variations of EKF-SLAM for Non-Linear Trends : First Estimates Jacobian EKF (FEJ-EKF), Iterated EKF
(IEKF), Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF), Submap-Based EKF-SLAM, Dynamic SLAM Variants.
Other such variations include a graph based SLAM or a Particle Filter Based SLAM involving algorithms like
Monte Carlo Simulations or a field based recursion.
You can refer to this article for more concreteness in EKF-SLAM -
https://jihongju.github.io/2018/10/05/slam-05-ekf-slam/

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7ESK5SaP-bc
And also explore this approach if interested in diving deeper into how SLAM utilises it:
https://robots.stanford.edu/papers/Thrun03g.pdf
4. Deep Learning in SLAM
Deep Learning (DL) has emerged as a transformative tool in Simultaneous Localization and Mapping
(SLAM), offering data-driven solutions to challenges that traditional geometry-based approaches struggle
with. By leveraging neural networks, SLAM systems can achieve greater robustness, adaptability, and
efficiency, especially in dynamic or complex environments. Below, we scratch the surface of this exciting
intersection by exploring key architectures, challenges, and their potential fixes.
Reinforcement learning can also see scope among the many existing algorithms for high speed localisation

C. Path Planning
1. What is Path Planning?
Path planning is a fundamental component of autonomous vehicle systems that determines a safe,
efficient, and feasible trajectory for a vehicle from its starting position to a desired destination. The primary
goal is to navigate through a dynamic environment while avoiding obstacles, adhering to traffic rules, and
optimizing factors such as time, energy, and comfort. It is the process of determining the optimal route
from a starting point to a destination while avoiding obstacles and adhering to rules and regulations.
2. Importance of Path Planning in Autonomous Vehicles
For autonomous vehicles, effective path planning is crucial because it directly impacts safety, efficiency,
and overall performance. A well-planned path ensures:
● Safety: Avoids collisions with static and dynamic obstacles.
● Efficiency: Finds the shortest or most optimal route based on criteria like travel time, energy
consumption, or passenger comfort.
● Adaptability: Responds to real-time changes in traffic, road conditions, or unpredictable obstacles.
Path planning can be broadly categorized into two types based on the level of planning and how dynamic
the environment is:
● Global planning: A high-level route between the source to destination based on the surroundings
and track of the autonomous vehicle. It assumes a known, static environment and does not
account for real-time changes such as moving obstacles or traffic conditions.
● Local planning: Handling real-time navigation and obstacle avoidance on the track. Unlike global
planning, as the name suggests, it reacts to changes in the environment as the vehicle moves.
3. Overview of Path Planning Algorithms

IIT Roorkee Motorsports Autonomous –
Recruitment 2026
I. Artificial Potential Field (APF)
The Artificial Potential Field (APF) method is a local path planning technique widely
used in autonomous vehicles and robotics for real-time obstacle avoidance. It models
the environment using a mathematical field where the goal exerts an attractive force,
and obstacles exert repulsive forces. The vehicle navigates by following the resultant
force, ensuring a smooth and safe trajectory.
Reference: Predictive Artificial Potential Field algorithm - energy-efficient local path
planning algorithm
II. Rapidly-Exploring Random Tree
This is a sampling-based method (in that it generates random
paths to find the optimal path). It allows efficient exploration of
high-dimensional spaces by incrementally building a tree
structure.
It consists of three steps, viz. exploration, obstacle avoidance
and path formation:
Reference: RRT* Algorithm Explained
III. A* and D* Algorithms
Both A* and D* are heuristic-based path planning algorithms widely used in robotics, AI, and autonomous
vehicles. A* is used for static environments, while D* is an extension designed for dynamic, changing
environments.
A* Algorithm
● A* is a heuristic-based graph-based search algorithm that finds the shortest path from a start node
to a goal node. It combines concepts from Dijkstra’s Algorithm (which finds the shortest path but
explores too many nodes) and Greedy Best-First Search (which is fast but may not always find the
best path)
D* (Dynamic A*) Algorithm
● D* is an extension of A* designed for dynamic environments where obstacles may change after
path computation. It is widely used in robotics and autonomous vehicles to replan paths when new
obstacles appear.
Reference: D* Lite Path Planning Algorithm, A* (A Star) Search Algorithm - Computerphile

IIT Roorkee Motorsports Autonomous –
Recruitment 2026
IV. Delaunay Triangulation and Voronoi Diagrams
Delaunay triangulation and Voronoi diagrams are geometric
structures used in computational geometry and robotics. They help
in path planning, obstacle avoidance, and spatial analysis in
autonomous vehicles in simple track situations like those in FS AI.
Voronoi Diagram
A Voronoi diagram partitions a plane into regions based on proximity
to a given set of points. Each region contains all points that are
closer to one specific point than to any other.
Delaunay Triangulation
Delaunay triangulation is a way of connecting a set of points with
non-overlapping triangles such that the circumcircle of each triangle
contains no other points. It is essentially equivalent to KNN with k=1
(ML algorithm).
V. Deep Learning Methods
RNNs and LSTMs are some architectures, alongside RL algorithms that can be used for various parts of
path planning. RL is increasingly used to replace the "Local Planner" or "Controller"—the component that
decides how to steer right now to stay on the optimal path calculated by the global planner.
1. Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) & TD3:
a. Role: Continuous control (steering angle and throttle/brake).
b. Application: These off-policy algorithms are sample-efficient and stable. They are trained
in simulators (like F1TENTH Gym or CARLA) to follow a racing line while minimizing lap
time.
c. Advantage: Unlike PID or pure pursuit, an RL agent can learn to "slip" the tires slightly or
brake late, exploiting vehicle dynamics that are hard to model mathematically.
2. Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO):
a. Role: Robust, on-policy control.
b. Application: Often used for obstacle avoidance (e.g., in the FSUK skidpad or trackdrive if
dynamic obstacles were introduced). It is easier to tune than SAC but typically requires
more training data.
We look forward to you being enthusiastic and interested in these type of methods to innovate and think
over the implications and usage of the current AI bubble to develop and research on these deep learning
based methods.

D. Control
1. Introduction
“How do we make the car follow the planned path accurately?”
Self-driving automobiles, sometimes referred to as autonomous vehicles, are a cutting-edge
technological advancement that could revolutionize our daily lives and modes of transportation. These
cars mimic a human driver’s decision-making process by utilizing a combination of sensors, software,
and control systems to make decisions in real time. They are designed to function alone and navigate.
Control theory is the study of how to apply force to an object to control its movement. For self-driving
cars, we can apply forces to the vehicle via three "control inputs": steering, throttle, and brake.
The algorithms that determine how to use these control inputs are called "controllers". There are many,
many types of controllers, although in the vehicle domain only a few are common and the rest are more
specialized. See references for more control algorithms.
● The control system receives a desired value, such as the target speed or steering angle, and also
measures the vehicle’s actual speed or steering using sensors.
● It compares the desired value with the actual value to calculate the error between them.
● A controller processes this error and decides how much steering, throttle, or braking action is
required.
● These control commands are sent to the vehicle, and the car moves according to physical effects
like tire grip and load transfer.
● Sensors measure the new vehicle state and send feedback to the controller, and this process
repeats continuously to keep the vehicle stable and accurate.

2. Open Loop v/s Closed Loop
● The above image was an example of an open-loop control system.
● The opposite of an open-loop configuration is a closed-loop system. This involves taking feedback
from the plant about its current state (via sensors), hence it is “closed”.
● While open-loop systems are easier to set up and implement, closed-loop systems provide better
tracking, especially with the presence of external disturbances, non-linearities, etc.
3. PID :

IIT Roorkee Motorsports Autonomous –
Recruitment 2026
● PID is one of the most commonly used and robust controllers out there. It stands for Proportional-
Integral-Derivative Controller. We have 3 constants here : namely K_p, K_i, K_d, each controlling the
proportional, integral, and derivative actions respectively.
● PID control is a feedback control method used to make a system reach and maintain a desired
value, such as speed or steering angle.
● The controller first calculates the error by comparing the desired value with the actual value
measured by sensors.
● The Proportional (P) part reacts to the current error and applies a correction that is directly
proportional to how big the error is.
● The Integral (I) part looks at the accumulated error over time and helps remove any steady-state
error that remains after proportional control.
● The Derivative (D) part predicts future errors by observing how fast the error is changing and
helps reduce overshoot and oscillations.
● The combined P, I, and D actions generate a control output that adjusts steering, throttle, or
braking.
● The controller continuously repeats this process using feedback, so the system remains stable
and accurate.

II. Types of Steering Systems
Parallel - Geometry -
The simplest steering system that can be designed is Parallel steering, which suggests that both the front
wheels will turn at an identical angle.
Ackermann - Geometry -
The typical steering system in a road car is the Ackermann steering system. It is governed by the
Ackermann principle, which prevents the tyres from slipping sideways while turning. To turn about an
instantaneous centre, the inner wheel angle is greater than the outer wheel angle to turn about an
instantaneous centre.
Anti-Ackerman Geometry -
Anti Ackermann geometry is the reverse of Ackermann geometry. The outer wheel angle is greater than
the inner wheel angle. This type of geometry is preferred by most racing cars.

4. Braking
Braking is the process of slowing down or stopping a vehicle. A hydraulic braking system (like the one used
in most cars with disc brakes) works as follows:
● Pressing the Brake Pedal
■ When you press the brake pedal, you apply a force.
■ This force is amplified by the pedal ratio (P) to make braking easier.
● Hydraulic System Activation
■ The force is transmitted to the master cylinder, which contains brake fluid.
■ This fluid gets compressed and travels through hydraulic lines to the brake calipers.
● Brake Caliper and Disc Interaction
■ The brake calipers have pistons that push brake pads against the rotating brake disc
(rotor).
■ This creates friction, which slows down the wheel.
● Stopping the Vehicle
■ The friction between the brake pads and disc produces braking torque (opposite to wheel
rotation).
■ This torque helps bring the vehicle to a stop.
● Releasing the Brakes
■ When you release the brake pedal, the pressure in the hydraulic system decreases.
■ The pistons move back, and the brake pads stop pressing the disc, allowing the wheels to
rotate freely.

2. Powertrain Configurations
Electric vehicles (EVs) use different motor configurations to power their wheels. Here's a simplified
explanation of three common setups:
I. Single Motor Configuration
Only one motor powers either the front or rear axle (axle: shaft connecting to both left and right wheels);
and uses a differential to distribute power and torque between the left and right wheel. This setup is simple
and cost-effective, ideal for everyday driving. However, it offers less control over traction and cornering.
● Simpler control algorithms focus on acceleration and braking.
● Limited capability for advanced traction control.
Differential: A differential allows wheels on the same axle to rotate at different speeds, improving cornering
by compensating for the varying distances each wheel travels during turns. Nowadays, there are
differentials that can be electronically and algorithmically controlled for torque/power distribution.

II. Dual Motor Configuration
Two independent motors, one for each wheel (left and right) on the same axle (either rear or front). This
setup provides better control and allows for torque vectoring on that axle.
● Improved traction during acceleration and enhanced handling.
● Algorithms focus on distributing power/torque between left and right motors.
● Better wheel slip detection and control for improved performance on slippery surfaces.
Torque vectoring: It dynamically adjusts power distribution between wheels, enhancing traction, stability,
and cornering in cars. For algorithm development in autonomous vehicles, it requires optimization of wheel-
specific torque based on real-time inputs from sensors.
III. Four Motor Configuration
One motor for each wheel, providing independent control of all four wheels. This setup offers the best
traction, cornering, and off-road performance.
● Ultimate precision in torque distribution, torque vectoring, performance and stability.
Besides, one can see how all 4 wheels’ motors have different torque distribution, while
cornering the car.

Motors in electric vehicles convert electrical energy from the battery into rotational mechanical energy.
This energy is then transmitted through the drivetrain, ultimately causing the wheels to rotate and propel
the vehicle forward. Unlike IC engines, electric motors provide very high efficiency in a broad range of speed
and torque, so most EVs don’t have multiple gear drives. Many kinds of motors, like BLDC, PMSM, and
Induction motors, are being used in various segments of EVs. PMSM motors generate more torque and
have higher efficiency, so they are used in most electric 4-wheelers nowadays.

I. Motor Control Unit
● Throttle input in vehicles translates to motor commands by interpreting the driver's acceleration
demand. Sensors capture the throttle position, and the Electronic Control Unit (ECU) processes the

● Further, the Motor Control Unit (MCU) transforms the battery’s direct current into alternating

G. Robot Operating System (ROS)
1. ROS and its use in Autonomous Vehicles:
ROS is a flexible framework that helps developers build robot applications. It is not actually an operating
system, rather a collection of tools, libraries, and conventions that simplify the complex task of creating
robust robot behaviors.
Autonomous vehicles need to handle multiple tasks simultaneously - they need to understand their
environment through sensors, plan their path, control their movement, and make split-second decisions.
ROS provides the infrastructure to manage all these tasks efficiently. For example, one component might
process camera data to detect obstacles, while another plans the safest route, and yet another controls
the steering and acceleration.
ROS2 is particularly well-suited for autonomous vehicles because it can handle the demanding
requirements of real-time processing and safety-critical operations. ROS2 has a highly sophisticated
modular software architecture where different functionalities (e.g., sensor fusion, decision-making, and
motion control) interact smoothly

Now, let's look at the core components of ROS2:
ROS2 follows a distributed computing model, where different components run as independent nodes.
These nodes communicate through:
● Topics → For continuous data streaming (e.g., LiDAR data).
● Services → For request-response operations.
● Actions → For long-running tasks like motion planning.
A term you will read a lot is the Publisher-Subscriber Mode
Message passing in ROS happens with the Publisher Subscriber Interface provided by ROS library
functions.
A ROS Node can be a Publisher or a Subscriber. A Publisher is the one that puts the messages of some
standard Message Type to a particular Topic. The Subscriber on the other hand subscribes to the Topic so
that it receives the messages whenever any message is published to the Topic.

IIT Roorkee Motorsports Autonomous –
Recruitment 2026
Also, publishers and subscribers are not aware of each others’ existence. The idea is to decouple the
production of information from its consumption and all the IP addresses of various nodes are tracked by
the ROS Master.
For SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping), different nodes handle sensor data processing, map
building, and position estimation. They communicate through topics to share sensor data and map
updates.
In perception, nodes process data from various sensors (cameras, lidar, radar) and publish their findings
on topics. Other nodes subscribe to this information to build a comprehensive understanding of the
environment.
Path planning uses this perceived information, subscribing to relevant topics to understand the
environment and obstacles. It then publishes planned paths that the control nodes can follow.

A. Perception
1. Understanding Images
In computer vision, an image can be either grayscale or color, depending on whether it contains information
about light intensity alone or the combination of different colors. The images are composed of pixels, where
each pixel has an intensity value. These images are stored as matrices, where:
● A grayscale image consists of a single channel of intensity values ranging from black to white. This
means it is represented as a 2D matrix with pixel values from 0(black) to 255(white).
● A color image has multiple channels, each representing a different color channel such as red, green,
and blue (RGB). They are represented by three 2D matrices, one for each color channel. Their
intensity values also vary from 0 to 255.
Some image characteristics are:
● Resolution: The resolution of an image refers to the number of pixels it contains. A higher
resolution image will contain more pixels and thus more detail.
● Bit Depth: Bit depth refers to the number of bits used to represent each pixel in an image. A higher
bit depth allows for more color and brightness variations in an image (since the number of possible
intensity values that can be represented increase).
● Noise: Noise refers to any unwanted artifacts or variations in an image. It can be caused by factors
such as sensor sensitivity, lighting conditions, or image compression.
For computers, understanding images requires mathematical operations on these matrices. These
mathematical operations help the computer in detecting edges, textures, corners etc. This is where
Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) come into play.
2. CNNs
As seen below, CNNs are neural networks where we have convolutional layers instead of fully connected
layers. Convolutional layers consist of “kernels,” which are matrices. The output of each layer is another
matrix known as feature maps, whose entries are calculated as the sum of input entries weighted by kernel
entries. The kernel sweeps over the entire input matrix (the image is represented as a matrix). In the case
of multiple channels, the weighted sums of each channel with its kernel are added to give output. The
convolutional layers learn features of images from simple things such as edges or some shapes to more
complicated things like facial features or complete objects.

IIT Roorkee Motorsports Autonomous –
Recruitment 2026
CNNs also consist of pooling layers and fully connected layers. Pooling layers are used to reduce the
dimensionality of feature maps. Fully connected layers are generally added at the network's end to predict
class probabilities in object identification tasks.
Most vision architectures use convolutions as a fundamental building block.

IIT Roorkee Motorsports Autonomous –
Recruitment 2026
3. Object Detection
Object detection involves identifying and localizing objects in an image. For localization, bounding boxes
are drawn around the identified object. Object detection algorithms employ a combination of techniques
to accomplish their tasks effectively. They utilize Region Proposal Networks (RPNs) to generate potential
object proposals. These proposals are then analyzed by feature extraction networks, which extract relevant
features to accurately classify and localize objects within the image or video.
Some popular object detection models include:
● Faster R-CNN (Region-based Convolutional Neural Network) : Uses a Region Proposal Network
(RPN) to efficiently detect objects with high accuracy.
● YOLO (You Only Look Once) : A fast and real-time object detection model that processes an image
in a single pass.
4. Object Segmentation
Object Segmentation or image segmentation refers to categorizing each pixel value of an image to a
particular class. This results in a pixel-wise mask for the objects. Types include:
● Semantic Segmentation: Classifies pixels into categories (e.g., road, car, pedestrian).
● Instance Segmentation: Differentiates between multiple instances of the same object (e.g., two
different cars).

IIT Roorkee Motorsports Autonomous –
Recruitment 2026
5. Object Tracking
Object tracking is the process of locating and following an object's movement in a video or a sequence of
frames. Object tracking aims to identify an object of interest in the first frame and track it as it moves in
subsequent frames. The object tracking process is terminated when the object of interest is no longer
visible or the tracking algorithm fails to track the object accurately.
It is crucial for autonomous driving to track objects that need to be avoided (like other cars, pedestrians, or
any other obstacles) or followed(lane boundaries need to be tracked to ensure the vehicle stays in the lane)
.
Some common problems in object racking include:

IIT Roorkee Motorsports Autonomous –
Recruitment 2026
● Occlusion: It is known as occlusion when the object is hidden partially or entirely due to other
objects. Solutions include using multiple detectors to detect different parts or a Kalman filter to
predict the expected position of the object based on its motion in the previous frame.
● Motion blur: Sometimes, objects can appear blurred in video frames due to fast motion. To handle
this Kalman filter can be used to track using the expected position until a sharp object is found
again.
6. LIDAR (Light Detection and Ranging) sensor
Cameras provide 2D images of the environment, but autonomous cars need 3D information like the
distance of objects from the vehicle or the velocity of other objects to make decisions, such as when to
turn or stop. LIDAR sensor is used for this purpose:
● Emits laser pulses and measures the reflected time to compute distance (like sonar, except it uses
light pulses instead of sound waves, which are significantly slower).
● Generates a 3D point cloud representing the surroundings.
● It helps in depth and velocity estimation, which is critical for navigation.
7. Stereo Camera

IIT Roorkee Motorsports Autonomous –
Recruitment 2026
A stereo camera is a type of camera with two or more image sensors. This allows the
camera to simulate human binocular vision and therefore gives it the ability to perceive
depth.
The human binocular vision perceives depth by using Stereo disparity which refers to
the difference in image location of an object seen by the left and right eyes, resulting
from the eyes’ horizontal separation.
The brain uses this binocular disparity to extract depth information from the two-
dimensional retinal images which are known as stereopsis.
Stereo Disparity in a camera can be found by using two 2D images taken from different
positions and the correlation between the images can be used to create a depth image.
However, to find correlations the two images need to have sufficient details and texture
or non-uniformity.
Thus, the Stereo vision is suitable for applications with a large field of view and for
outdoor usage.

ORB is a fast, efficient algorithm designed to detect "keypoints" (interesting features) in

created as a computationally efficient, open-source alternative to SIFT and SURF.
ORB is actually a fusion of two other algorithms, modified to handle rotation:
1. Feature Detector: Oriented FAST (oFAST)
2. Feature Descriptor: Rotated BRIEF (rBRIEF)

B. SLAM
1. Introduction to SLAM
https://www.mathworks.com/discovery/slam.html
https://www.mdpi.com/2072-4292/15/4/1156 (Intensive one)
Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) is a groundbreaking technology that allows autonomous
systems, such as robots or vehicles, to navigate and understand unfamiliar environments. It achieves this
by simultaneously creating a map of the surroundings and determining the system's position within that
map. This dual challenge was once considered a "chicken-or-egg" problem in robotics, as mapping requires
knowing the location, and localization requires a map. SLAM elegantly solves this interdependency.
In essence, SLAM enables machines to explore unknown spaces much like humans do when navigating
new places by identifying landmarks and using them as reference points.
For example:
● A robotic vacuum cleaner uses SLAM to map your house while cleaning, ensuring it doesn’t miss
spots or repeatedly clean the same area.
● A self-driving car uses SLAM to navigate roads safely by continuously updating its map of the
surroundings.

IIT Roorkee Motorsports Autonomous –
Recruitment 2026
2. Core Components of SLAM
SLAM systems are built on two essential pillars:
I. Range-Bearing Measurement & Prediction
Every SLAM system relies on a sensing mechanism that enables a robot or vehicle to perceive and measure
its surroundings. This is achieved through various technologies, including cameras, image sensors, LiDAR
(Light Detection and Ranging), and sonar. Essentially, any device capable of detecting spatial properties
such as location, distance, and velocity can serve as a range measurement tool within a SLAM framework.
II. Data Processing and Extraction
Once environmental measurements are collected, the SLAM system employs computational methods to
process and interpret this data. The type of data ranges from the accelerometer readings from the car’s
body to the positional observance of objects surrounding the vehicle. The primary objective of this data
extraction process is to obtain the necessary data to help aid the calculations of position, acceleration,
velocity and yaw.
A well-integrated SLAM system continuously synchronizes its sensing hardware, data processing
algorithms, and the movement of the robot or vehicle itself. This interplay ensures that the system can
accurately explore and navigate its surroundings. While the specific components and technologies used
may vary depending on the application, their seamless coordination is essential for the system’s
effectiveness.
III. Data Association and Mapping
Reference: https://web.mit.edu/2.166/www/handouts/2166_data_association_jose_neira.pdf
Data Association is one of the key features governing a SLAM algorithm. To accurately map and recognise
its own position within the virtual world, the algorithm must relate each new observed frame with the
preceding one based on estimates to determine where the vehicle should be after the duration of time
between the observed frames. Many such algorithms even accommodate the use of gating regions, in an
attempt to simplify the association calculations.

previously visited areas and correcting any drift in localization or mapping errors.
For example: Imagine a robot exploring a building. When it revisits a hallway it mapped earlier, loop closure
ensures that any discrepancies between its current position and the earlier map are corrected.

Steps in LiDAR SLAM for Cone Detection
● Preprocessing Point Cloud Data:
■ Field of View Trimming: Limits the sensor's range to focus only on relevant areas.

detection.
● Clustering Potential Obstacles:
■ Groups point in the cloud that likely belong to the same object (e.g., a cone).
● Key Techniques:
■ Cylindrical Volume Reconstruction: Ensures all points within a cylindrical region around
each cluster are included.
■ Filtering Based on Expected Point Counts: Verifies if the number of points in a cluster
matches the expected size of a cone.
● Scan Matching Algorithms:
■ These algorithms align consecutive LiDAR scans to maintain accurate localization:
■ - Iterative Closest Point (ICP): Matches corresponding points between scans to minimize
positional errors.

● Wheel odometry provides initial estimates of the vehicle's movement based on wheel rotations.

IV. EKF-SLAM
EKF-SLAM estimates both the robot's position and the locations of landmarks (e.g., cones) in the

to process noise.
● Correction: Updates the prediction using observed landmarks, reducing uncertainty by comparing
predicted and actual sensor measurements.
For example, a robot navigating a cone-marked track predicts its movement using odometry and refines its
position when it observes a cone landmark.

C. Path Planning
1. What is Path Planning?
Path planning is a fundamental component of autonomous vehicle systems that determines a safe,
efficient, and feasible trajectory for a vehicle from its starting position to a desired destination. The primary
goal is to navigate through a dynamic environment while avoiding obstacles, adhering to traffic rules, and
optimizing factors such as time, energy, and comfort. It is the process of determining the optimal route
from a starting point to a destination while avoiding obstacles and adhering to rules and regulations.
2. Importance of Path Planning in Autonomous Vehicles
For autonomous vehicles, effective path planning is crucial because it directly impacts safety, efficiency,
and overall performance. A well-planned path ensures:
● Safety: Avoids collisions with static and dynamic obstacles.
● Efficiency: Finds the shortest or most optimal route based on criteria like travel time, energy
consumption, or passenger comfort.
● Adaptability: Responds to real-time changes in traffic, road conditions, or unpredictable obstacles.
Path planning can be broadly categorized into two types based on the level of planning and how dynamic
the environment is:
● Global planning: A high-level route between the source to destination based on the surroundings
and track of the autonomous vehicle. It assumes a known, static environment and does not
account for real-time changes such as moving obstacles or traffic conditions.
● Local planning: Handling real-time navigation and obstacle avoidance on the track. Unlike global
planning, as the name suggests, it reacts to changes in the environment as the vehicle moves.
3. Overview of Path Planning Algorithms

I. Artificial Potential Field (APF)
The Artificial Potential Field (APF) method is a local path planning technique widely
used in autonomous vehicles and robotics for real-time obstacle avoidance. It models
the environment using a mathematical field where the goal exerts an attractive force,
and obstacles exert repulsive forces. The vehicle navigates by following the resultant
force, ensuring a smooth and safe trajectory.
Reference: Predictive Artificial Potential Field algorithm - energy-efficient local path
planning algorithm
II. Rapidly-Exploring Random Tree
This is a sampling-based method (in that it generates random
paths to find the optimal path). It allows efficient exploration of
high-dimensional spaces by incrementally building a tree
structure.
It consists of three steps, viz. exploration, obstacle avoidance
and path formation:
Reference: RRT* Algorithm Explained
III. A* and D* Algorithms
Both A* and D* are heuristic-based path planning algorithms widely used in robotics, AI, and autonomous
vehicles. A* is used for static environments, while D* is an extension designed for dynamic, changing
environments.
A* Algorithm
● A* is a heuristic-based graph-based search algorithm that finds the shortest path from a start node
to a goal node. It combines concepts from Dijkstra’s Algorithm (which finds the shortest path but
explores too many nodes) and Greedy Best-First Search (which is fast but may not always find the
best path)
D* (Dynamic A*) Algorithm
● D* is an extension of A* designed for dynamic environments where obstacles may change after
path computation. It is widely used in robotics and autonomous vehicles to replan paths when new
obstacles appear.
Reference: D* Lite Path Planning Algorithm, A* (A Star) Search Algorithm - Computerphile

IV. Delaunay Triangulation and Voronoi Diagrams
Delaunay triangulation and Voronoi diagrams are geometric
structures used in computational geometry and robotics. They help
in path planning, obstacle avoidance, and spatial analysis in
autonomous vehicles in simple track situations like those in FS AI.
Voronoi Diagram
A Voronoi diagram partitions a plane into regions based on proximity
to a given set of points. Each region contains all points that are
closer to one specific point than to any other.
Delaunay Triangulation
Delaunay triangulation is a way of connecting a set of points with
non-overlapping triangles such that the circumcircle of each triangle
contains no other points. It is essentially equivalent to KNN with k=1
(ML algorithm).
V. Deep Learning Methods
RNNs and LSTMs are some architectures, alongside RL algorithms that can be used for various parts of
path planning. RL is increasingly used to replace the "Local Planner" or "Controller"—the component that
decides how to steer right now to stay on the optimal path calculated by the global planner.
1. Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) & TD3:
a. Role: Continuous control (steering angle and throttle/brake).
b. Application: These off-policy algorithms are sample-efficient and stable. They are trained
in simulators (like F1TENTH Gym or CARLA) to follow a racing line while minimizing lap
time.
c. Advantage: Unlike PID or pure pursuit, an RL agent can learn to "slip" the tires slightly or
brake late, exploiting vehicle dynamics that are hard to model mathematically.
2. Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO):
a. Role: Robust, on-policy control.
b. Application: Often used for obstacle avoidance (e.g., in the FSUK skidpad or trackdrive if
dynamic obstacles were introduced). It is easier to tune than SAC but typically requires
more training data.
